name: Redis — LPUSH (single node, non-pipelined)
notes: redis-benchmark LPUSH against one single-node Redis at the tool's default configuration (50 parallel clients, 3-byte payload, no pipelining), from the SAME `redis-benchmark -t set,lpush -n 100000 -q` run as the SET entry. Modeled identically — SDA's cache.redis does not distinguish commands, so both are the same single-node architecture under a different command workload (the exact analogue of TechEmpower single-query vs 20-query: one architecture, two workloads, one shared tunable). The shared tunable cache.redis:throughput makes the fit over-determined; the leave-one-out (fit on SET, predict LPUSH) is a genuine out-of-sample test. Same config-sensitivity caveat as the SET entry — the ceiling is validated, not the tail.

sources.note: Official redis-benchmark documentation. `redis-benchmark -t set,lpush -n 100000 -q` reports `LPUSH: 188323.91 requests per second, p50=0.135 msec`. Default config: 50 parallel clients, 3-byte payload, no pipelining, single instance.
Redis FAQ: 'Redis is, mostly, a single-threaded server from the POV of command execution' — the sourced basis for the single-node M/M/1 model.

tunables.note: The SAME free variable as the SET entry (selector cache.redis, key throughput) — one shared Redis single-node capacity. Sharing it across the two commands is what over-determines the fit and makes the leave-one-out a genuine out-of-sample test of the single-node model.

groundTruth.note: `redis-benchmark -t set,lpush -n 100000 -q` -> `LPUSH: 188323.91 requests per second, p50=0.135 msec` (default non-pipelined config). LPUSH is marginally faster than SET on the single-threaded loop; SDA models both with ONE capacity, so the ~4.5% SET/LPUSH spread is the irreducible residual a single capacity value cannot capture.
